network.overall_strategy: This network builds threonine through literature-grounded route classes rather than a single asserted threonine-specific mechanism, because the research record indicates that exact threonine-forming prebiotic sequences remain unresolved. The network therefore centers on experimentally discussed precursor families and environments: hydrothermal generation of reduced C1/C2 feedstocks and protometabolic keto-acid hubs, surface Strecker and phosphoro-Strecker chemistry from aldehydes with HCN/NH3/DAP, cyanosulfidic production of hydroxy-nitrile intermediates, and biochemical/prebiotic completion by hydrolysis, transamination analogs, and wet-dry concentration. The threonine-specific branch uses 2-hydroxypropanal as the most chemically appropriate hydroxyaldehyde precursor for threonine-forming Strecker/phosphoro-Strecker chemistry, while explicitly noting that direct threonine-specific experimental proof is limited. Ten distinct pathways are provided as alternative traversals through the same connected bipartite network.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface reduction of CO2 by H2 to surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents followed by release as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on Fe-S minerals is a core vent-chemistry motif and provides the C1 feedstock used in later surface chemistry; this step follows the hydrothermal carbon-fixation framework emphasized for early metabolism and alkaline vent systems (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Hall, 1997).

reactions.name: Magnetite-mediated Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of formaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal synthesis; reduced vent fluids

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type reduction and chain-growth chemistry from CO2/CO-derived intermediates produces small aldehydes including formaldehyde and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite is the best-supported specific mineral catalyst in the supplied literature for FTT-type prebiotic organic synthesis, and small aldehydes are recognized precursor classes for Strecker chemistry relevant to amino acids including threonine precursors (Horita & Berndt, 1999; McCollom, 2013; Wolman et al., 1971).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic formation of glyoxylate and pyruvate

conditions.other: anoxic vent conditions; protometabolic carbon-fixation context

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-supported reductive carboxylation and carbonyl coupling produce α-ketoacid hubs, represented here by glyoxylate and pyruvate, from simple fixed-carbon vent products.
reactions.reasoning: The knowledge base identifies glyoxylate and pyruvate as prebiotically relevant protometabolic hubs connected to amino-acid formation. Exact threonine-specific yields are not available, but inclusion of these α-ketoacids is literature-grounded and avoids unsupported direct one-step threonine formation (Stubbs et al., 2020; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019).

reactions.name: Surface cyanide generation from reduced C1 feedstocks

conditions.other: UV irradiation, anoxic surface pools; product set represents reduced nitrile/formamide feedstocks rather than a single mandatory stoichiometric channel

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical conversion of simple carbonyl/nitrogen feedstocks generates nitrile chemistry and formamide-rich mixtures that seed Strecker and UV-driven prebiotic networks.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report specifically links HCN, aldehydes, ammonia, UV chemistry, and formamide to prebiotic amino-acid and nucleobase synthesis, while also noting uncertainty in exact environmental coupling. This reaction is included as a connecting feedstock-generation step and is explicitly somewhat schematic due to limited threonine-specific mechanistic resolution (Saladino et al., 2012; Sutherland, 2016; Wolman et al., 1971).

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide to formaldehyde gives the cyanohydrin glycolonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolonitrile is identified in the supplied research as a central cyanosulfidic precursor and feedstock intermediate relevant to amino-acid-forming networks. This is a well-established prebiotic cyanohydrin step (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Oxidation and homologation from glycolonitrile to hydroxypyruvate and hydroxyaldehyde pool

conditions.other: UV photochemistry with borate stabilization in evaporitic pools

reactions.mechanism: Cyanosulfidic and carbonyl-homologation chemistry transforms cyanohydrins and carbonyl compounds into more oxidized C3 hydroxy-carbonyl species, represented by hydroxypyruvate and lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies glycolonitrile-derived chemistry, hydroxy-sulfonate/cyanosulfidic intermediates, and hydroxy-carbonyl precursors as relevant to amino-acid synthesis. Exact threonine-specific conversion to 2-hydroxypropanal is not directly demonstrated in the supplied literature, so this step is explicitly a necessary but partially speculative connector grounded in the general cyanosulfidic framework (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018; Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Aldol-type formation of 2-hydroxypropanal from acetaldehyde and formaldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling enhances concentration of carbonyl substrates

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl homologation/aldol-like coupling among small aldehydes furnishes a hydroxyaldehyde pool including 2-hydroxypropanal.
reactions.reasoning: The literature highlights aldehydes, formaldehyde, and surface chemistry as relevant feedstocks for amino-acid synthesis, but does not provide a threonine-specific experimental route to 2-hydroxypropanal. This reaction is therefore marked as a constrained connector: it supplies the chemically appropriate hydroxyaldehyde precursor required for threonine-specific Strecker logic while remaining within the documented aldehyde/hydroxyaldehyde framework (Butlerow, 1861; Ricardo et al., 2004; Wolman et al., 1971).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface oxidation of pyruvate to hydroxypyruvate equivalents

conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow waters; cross-environment transfer from hydrothermal keto acids

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation/rearrangement of protometabolic α-ketoacid pools gives hydroxy-keto intermediates feeding hydroxyaldehyde chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The report emphasizes α-ketoacids such as pyruvate and glyoxylate as key prebiotic intermediates and notes surface/UV chemistry as relevant. This connector is included to couple hydrothermal protometabolism with surface hydroxy-carbonyl chemistry; it is plausible but less directly demonstrated than Strecker chemistry itself (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019; Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from 2-hydroxypropanal

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous pools; aldehyde-selective aminonitrile formation

reactions.mechanism: Imine formation between the hydroxyaldehyde and ammonia followed by cyanide addition yields the α-aminonitrile corresponding to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker reaction is one of the most strongly supported prebiotic amino-acid syntheses in the supplied literature. Applying it to a hydroxyaldehyde gives the correct threonine carbon skeleton through the aminonitrile intermediate. The route class is literature-supported, although direct threonine-specific yield data remain limited (Strecker, 1850; Cleaves, 2010; Powner et al., 2009 for prebiotic nitrile chemistry context).

reactions.name: Phosphoro-Strecker formation of N-phosphoro-threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Diamidophosphate activates the aldehyde/HCN manifold to form an N-phosphoroaminonitrile under neutral conditions.
reactions.reasoning: This follows the phosphoro-Strecker chemistry identified explicitly in the research summary: aldehyde + DAP + HCN at pH 7 gives N-phosphoroaminonitriles selectively from aldehydes. Using 2-hydroxypropanal supplies the threonine carbon skeleton while retaining the documented route class (Krishnamurthy et al., 2018; Muchowska et al., 2020 as broader phosphate-enabled prebiotic context).

reactions.name: H2S-mediated conversion of N-phosphoroaminonitrile to aminothioamide
reactions.mechanism: Sulfur-mediated conversion of the phosphoroaminonitrile to the corresponding aminothioamide.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature specifically states that phosphoro-Strecker intermediates react with H2S at pH 7 to form aminothioamides. This is one of the most direct literature-grounded steps in the network (Krishnamurthy et al., 2018; Gibard et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Acidic conversion of phosphoroaminonitrile to free aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: pH-triggered dephosphorylative conversion of the N-phosphoroaminonitrile to the corresponding aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step directly follows the literature summary: at pH 1.5, phosphoro-Strecker intermediates furnish aminonitriles. It is retained as a distinct node to avoid the overly compressed endpoint criticized in earlier attempts (Krishnamurthy et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling or prolonged residence in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration and hydrolysis produces the α-amino acid threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the canonical completion step of Strecker chemistry. The literature summary explicitly identifies aminonitriles as threonine-relevant intermediates and hydrolysis as their productive fate (Strecker, 1850; Cleaves, 2010; Kitadai & Maruyama, 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis and deprotection of threonine aminothioamide to threonine

conditions.other: wet-dry or pH-cycling conditions in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Aminothioamide hydrolysis yields the corresponding amino acid under prolonged aqueous processing.
reactions.reasoning: The phosphoro-Strecker literature identifies aminothioamides as productive downstream intermediates. Their hydrolytic conversion is a reasonable prebiotic completion route and is more faithful to the reported sequence than collapsing H2S and hydrolysis into one step (Krishnamurthy et al., 2018; Gibard et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Abiotic keto-acid route to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate

conditions.other: concentrated pool chemistry; non-enzymatic carbonyl addition/transformation

reactions.mechanism: Non-enzymatic carbonyl coupling of a hydroxy-ketoacid equivalent with acetaldehyde gives the C4 α-ketoacid precursor 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate.
reactions.reasoning: The report highlights abiotic conversion of α-ketoacids as a plausible prebiotic route class and emphasizes glyoxylate/pyruvate-centered protometabolism. This threonine-specific C4 ketoacid step is more speculative than Strecker chemistry, but it is included because the literature explicitly points to α-ketoacid-to-amino-acid analogs as relevant and because it provides an alternative convergent route without compressing directly to threonine (Kitadai et al., 2018; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019).

reactions.name: Abiotic amination/transamination analog from 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous phosphate-rich pools; NH3/CN-assisted imine chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Imine formation at the α-ketoacid followed by reductive/assisted amination gives the corresponding amino acid threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The knowledge base specifically identifies abiotic conversion of α-ketoacids with cyanide and ammonia as a proposed amino-acid-forming framework mirroring biological transamination. Direct demonstration for threonine is limited, so this endpoint is explicitly a plausible analog rather than a fully established threonine-specific experiment (Kitadai et al., 2018; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019).

pathways.name: Direct hydroxyaldehyde Strecker from hydrothermal aldehydes
pathways.description: Hydrothermal aldehydes feed surface hydroxyaldehyde formation, followed by Strecker aminonitrile synthesis and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile route to Strecker threonine
pathways.description: Surface photochemistry converts hydrothermal formaldehyde into glycolonitrile and then a hydroxyaldehyde pool that enters Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Phosphoro-Strecker to aminonitrile then hydrolysis
pathways.description: Hydroxyaldehyde precursor enters phosphoro-Strecker chemistry, then acidic conversion gives the free aminonitrile which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Phosphoro-Strecker via aminothioamide
pathways.description: The phosphoro-Strecker intermediate is diverted through the H2S-mediated aminothioamide route before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic keto-acid route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation yields glyoxylate and pyruvate, which feed a hydroxy-ketoacid branch to a C4 ketoacid and then abiotic amination to threonine.

pathways.name: Mixed aldehyde and keto-acid convergence
pathways.description: Hydrothermal aldehydes generate acetaldehyde while hydrothermal protometabolism supplies hydroxypyruvate; these converge on 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate before amination.

pathways.name: Feedstock-to-HCN-to-Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formaldehyde and ammonia give surface cyanide/formamide feedstocks, which are then used for Strecker synthesis of the threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Feedstock-to-HCN-to-phosphoro-Strecker route
pathways.description: The same hydrothermal-to-surface feedstock transfer supplies cyanide for phosphoro-Strecker formation and aminonitrile hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic to phosphoro-Strecker hybrid
pathways.description: Glycolonitrile-based surface chemistry furnishes the hydroxyaldehyde precursor, which then enters phosphoro-Strecker chemistry and the aminothioamide branch.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical fully integrated route
pathways.description: Vent carbon fixation supplies formate, glyoxylate, and pyruvate; surface UV chemistry furnishes HCN and hydroxyaldehydes; both streams converge through Strecker chemistry to threonine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest route in the network because it follows a recognizable prebiotic logic: hydrothermal generation of aldehyde feedstocks, surface Strecker conversion of a hydroxyaldehyde, then hydrolysis to threonine. The main weakness is rxn_007, where direct aldol-type access to lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde and formaldehyde is plausible only in a broad sense and is not well established as a selective prebiotic route. The final Strecker/hydrolysis logic is literature-aligned, though threonine-specific selectivity remains uncertain.
suggestions: Specify mineral agents and pH for rxn_007 to improve plausibility
Clarify whether mol_013 is specifically 2-hydroxypropanal and address regio/stereochemical mixture issues
Include literature citations for the Strecker step and aminonitrile hydrolysis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway benefits from literature support for glycolonitrile as a key cyanosulfidic intermediate and for Strecker chemistry generally. However, rxn_004 is chemically questionable because formaldehyde plus ammonia is not a convincing direct route to HCN and formamide under stated surface conditions. rxn_006 is also weak because simultaneous oxidation/homologation from glycolonitrile to both hydroxypyruvate and lactaldehyde compresses multiple poorly justified steps.
suggestions: Replace rxn_004 with a more plausible HCN source or treat HCN as exogenous input
Split rxn_006 into separately justified transformations
Anchor the cyanosulfidic branch to documented sulfide/UV conditions and explicit mineral agents

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway aligns well with literature emphasizing phosphoro-Strecker chemistry as a plausible prebiotic route to amino acid precursors, including threonine-class products from aldehydes. The strongest issue remains the upstream generation of mol_013 via rxn_007. Once the hydroxyaldehyde is accepted, the DAP/HCN branch and acidic dephosphorylation to aminonitrile are well motivated by the literature summary.
suggestions: Add explicit pH sequence for phosphoro-Strecker steps consistent with reported literature
Clarify whether phosphate minerals or DAP formation/source are environmentally integrated
Support rxn_007 with a more specific prebiotic aldol/hydroxyaldehyde study

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is another relatively strong pathway. The phosphoro-Strecker to aminothioamide conversion is literature-aligned, and H2S-mediated processing of N-phosphoroaminonitriles is one of the more specifically supported parts of the network. The weakest feature is still the assumed prebiotic access to lactaldehyde from rxn_007. Final conversion of aminothioamide to threonine is plausible but should ideally specify hydrolysis conditions more clearly.
suggestions: Specify pH and aqueous conditions for aminothioamide formation and hydrolysis
Document the environmental source and persistence of DAP
Reduce reliance on the least supported hydroxyaldehyde-forming step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest pathway. It attempts to mirror protometabolic keto-acid logic, which is literature-relevant in a general sense, but the specific transformations to threonine are highly speculative. rxn_003 compresses difficult chemistry into a single step and appears to require acetate as an undefined precursor in the pathway context. rxn_008, rxn_015, and especially rxn_016 lack convincing prebiotic precedent for selective threonine formation from these intermediates.
suggestions: Treat the keto-acid route as speculative and downgrade confidence explicitly
Provide a defined source for acetate and justify carbon accounting more carefully
Replace rxn_016 with a literature-supported aminonitrile or reductive amination sequence

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This mixed route gains some robustness from ending in a standard Strecker/hydrolysis sequence, but it is weakened by combining two speculative upstream branches. The keto-acid oxidation branch is poorly justified, and the aldehyde branch still depends on the uncertain aldol access to lactaldehyde. As a result, convergence exists topologically, but not strongly chemically.
suggestions: Use convergence on a better-supported intermediate such as glycolonitrile or a phosphoroaminonitrile
Simplify the pathway by removing speculative oxidation-equivalent steps
Add explicit mineral agents and literature support for the surface homologation chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is conceptually coherent as a feedstock-to-HCN-to-Strecker pathway, which matches broad literature themes. Still, it inherits two important weaknesses: direct HCN generation in rxn_004 is not well justified, and hydroxyaldehyde formation in rxn_007 is not clearly established. The terminal chemistry remains plausible.
suggestions: Use an externally supplied HCN pool or a more plausible geochemical cyanide source
Support hydroxyaldehyde synthesis with a documented cyanohydrin-based or aldol-derived route
Distinguish broad plausibility from experimentally demonstrated selectivity

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is stronger than the ordinary Strecker version because the phosphoro-Strecker core is better aligned with current literature than the improvised HCN-production and aldehyde-generation steps feeding it. It nearly reaches approval but is held back by the same uncertain upstream chemistry.
suggestions: Keep the phosphoro-Strecker branch but revise the source of HCN and mol_013
State pH 7, pH 1.5, and related literature-derived conditions explicitly
Provide citations for DAP-mediated selectivity toward aldehydes

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hybrid route combines two literature-relevant motifs: cyanosulfidic chemistry and phosphoro-Strecker/thioamide processing. Its principal flaw is the unsupported jump in rxn_006 from glycolonitrile to both hydroxypyruvate and lactaldehyde equivalents. Downstream steps are relatively credible.
suggestions: Replace rxn_006 with literature-grounded cyanohydrin or bisulfite-derived intermediates
Better separate cyanosulfidic precursor generation from phosphoro-Strecker execution
Add UV, sulfide mineral, and pH details to improve surface chemistry realism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best topological integration of the whole network, showing hydrothermal-to-surface-to-biochemical flow clearly and in line with the requested design logic. However, the pathway still depends on rxn_006, which is not convincingly decomposed into realistic prebiotic chemistry. With that repaired, this would likely be the best overall route.
suggestions: Retain the integrated architecture but replace rxn_006 with a sequence of experimentally grounded steps
Add explicit mineral agents for every hydrothermal and surface step
Include literature citations in each reaction reasoning field, especially for phosphoro-Strecker chemistry

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong: it has ample node count, clear hubs, multiple convergence points, and a connected hydrothermal-surface-biochemical architecture. It also aligns reasonably well with the literature at the route-class level, especially by emphasizing Strecker, phosphoro-Strecker, cyanosulfidic precursors, and the unresolved status of threonine-specific prebiotic synthesis. However, overall confidence is reduced by several chemically weak or over-compressed reactions. The major issues are: (1) implausible direct generation of HCN/formamide from formaldehyde plus ammonia in rxn_004; (2) an under-justified homologation/oxidation step in rxn_006; (3) uncertain selective formation of lactaldehyde in rxn_007; and (4) a highly speculative keto-acid branch in rxn_015-rxn_016. Environmental interconnection is good in concept, but the supplied summary does not show whether every hydrothermal and surface reaction actually specified required mineral agents and literature citations in the full schema. Literature alignment is moderate rather than strong: the network follows known pathway families, but some reaction-specific implementations are not clearly evidenced by the provided research. Overall, this is a good but not fully viable network without revision of the weakest transformations.
